attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with acute onset shortness of breath, haemoptysis and chest pain. In the preceding days she noted warmth and tenderness in her left calf. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She had recently returned from a long haul flight from Europe.

Computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA) confirms a pulmonary embolism and she is started on intravenous heparin initially while her haemoptysis is ongoing. Once started on heparin, her condition begins to improve, however routine bloods on the following days, show her platelet count falling gradually from 200x109 /L (150-450) on day one to 90x109 /L on day six.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Switch to enoxaparin (low molecular weight heparin)
Change heparin to direct thrombin inhibitor immediately
Give a dose of antithrombin III concentrate, continue Heparin infusion
attributes.explanation:
Heparin is commonly used as an anticoagulant in cases where there is an acute high risk of bleeding. This is because of the comparatively short half-life of heparin compared to other anticoagulants, and because of a somewhat accessible reversal agent (protamine). In this case it was used because of the patient's ongoing haemoptysis.

In this case the patient is suffering from Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), a life-threatening, autoimmune complication of exposure to heparin. HIT results from the production of autoantibodies directed against platelet factor 4 in complex with heparin. This leads to the activation of platelets and thrombocytopenia, as well as catastrophic arterial and venous thrombosis.

Immediate cessation of heparin and changing to a non-heparin anticoagulant such as direct thrombin inhibitor (i.e. lepirudin, danaparoid or argatroban) is the most important aspect of management of HIT. Patients with HIT must continue a form of anticoagulation as the condition paradoxically produces a prothrombotic state so therapeutic anticoagulation is necessary, even if the patient does not have a thrombus at the time of diagnosis.

The clinical presentation of HIT is variable and the condition may occur with any heparin dose, schedule or route of administration, however, there is a greater incidence of HIT in patients receiving unfractionated heparin, as opposed to low molecular weight heparin. HIT typically occurs 5-10 days after the initiation of heparin, while clinical manifestations may include haemorrhage, thrombosis, as well as sequelae of thrombosis such as skin necrosis, limb gangrene or organ ischaemia.

Diagnosis of HIT is based on clinical and laboratory findings and should be suspected in a heparinised patient with new-onset thrombocytopenia or the clinical symptoms of HIT. The 4Ts score may be used to calculate the pretest probability of HIT. It includes:

  • Thrombocytopenia

  • Timing after heparin exposure [5-10 days]

  • Thrombosis or other clinical features

  • No other cause of thrombocytopenia

Laboratory testing includes two major types of antibody tests, immunoassays and functional assays. Immunoassays are the most widely used and detect the presence of the platelet factor 4-heparin antibody. Functional assays detect the ability of the antibody to bind and activate platelets; they are more resource-intensive however carry a higher specificity than immunoassays. The serotonin release assay is the gold standard for the diagnosis of HIT, it measures platelet activation by detecting the release of serotonin from platelets. Antibody testing is widely available but the serotonin release assay is only performed at a few laboratories in Australia. Therefore antibody testing is performed routinely to investigate HIT and the serotonin release assay is reserved for confirmatory testing in cases without conclusive antibody test results.

Treatment of HIT should involve the cessation of all formulations of heparin and careful monitoring of clinical features and platelet count. Alternative anticoagulation should be initiated immediately, options include fondaparinux or argatroban in the acute setting, followed by transition to a DOAC (Direct-Acting Oral Anticoagulant) or warfarin. Warfarin should not be used until the platelet count has normalised as it may exacerbate the imbalance between anticoagulant and procoagulant proteins seen in HIT.

Since this particular patient has only just been diagnosed with a pulmonary embolus, it is essential that some form of anticoagulation is maintained (ie. cease all anticoagulation is incorrect). Whilst Enoxaparin has a lower risk of causing or perpetuating HIT than unfractionated Heparin, it still is a cause of HIT and must also be avoided indefinitely (lifelong).

Placing an inferior vena cava filter may be useful in individuals who cannot have anticoagulation for an extended period or with recurrent thromboembolic events, but it is not appropriate in this case. An urgent platelet transfusion without ceasing heparin would only briefly raise the platelet count and is not indicated at this point as there is no evidence of haemorrhage and the thrombocytopaenia is not severe.

The main cause for heparin resistance is deficiency of Antithrombin III. Transfusing antithrombin concentrate will correct such a deficiency and enable the anticoagulant effect of heparin. However, heparin resistance does not cause thrombocytopaenia so it is unlikely to be an issue in this scenario.
learningPoints: Development of heparin-induced thrombocytopaenia demands immediate cessation of heparin and management with a non-heparin anticoagulant, often a direct thrombin inhibitor (i.e. Argatroban) or Fondaparinux
references: Ahmed I, Majeed A, Powell R. Heparin induced thrombocytopenia: diagnosis and management update. Postgrad Med J. 2007;83(983):575-82. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2600013/

Cuker A, Arepally GM, Chong BH, Cines DB, Greinacher A, Gruel Y, et al. American Society of Hematology 2018 guidelines for management of venous thromboembolism: heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Blood Adv. 2018;2(22):3360-92. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6258919/
Lee GM, Arepally GM. Diagnosis and management of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Hematol Oncol Clin North Am. 2013;27(3):541-63. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3668315/

Nagler M, Bachmann LM, ten Cate H, ten Cate-Hoek A. Diagnostic value of immunoassays for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Blood. 2016;127(5):546-57. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26518436/
